sentences.text: Yes, with the Brazilian embassy.
He talked these things over at our family table.
He had long talks.
And gradually, I learned a lot of German from being here and listening to their talks and asking questions and being told quite a little.

And this German group, German consular group in Berlin, thought that it would be very nice if he would set up a tablecloth factory, and the Silesian weavers and textile makers were-- were really very famous.

And if he would bring his know how to Brazilian, to Brazil, and perhaps be able to bring from Hitler-- I say Hitler, I mean from Germany, of course-- a certain amount of capital, they too, the Brazilians, would invest in that because it looked like a flourishing possibility.
He had tremendous know how.
It was one of the most prospering textile plants in all Germany.
He told me proudly once when he came back from Berlin, 'I just got a credit for $500,000.' Marks, I should say, [INAUDIBLE].
And he didn't need the credit.
He didn't have a factory.
But it showed that he was a man of-- you know, he was a financial man.
And that was part of his accomplishment in life, you know.
And-- but this didn't materialize, and it was very late in the day.
It was-- When I left there, it was spring '39.
And of course, we know the war started in September.
But stupid Jane Lester didn't know that.
I tell you, I had a good American education, and they taught me that Wilson had said World War I was the war to end all wars.
And anything that Hitler did or the armies marching, that was just-- that didn't mean anything because no fool would start another war.
It was-- you couldn't be that stupid, even Hitler, to want to start another war.
And so I didn't register that we were coming right up close to a war.
Not like my mentor, Robert Kempner.
He already knew long before that a war was coming.
He always said, Hitler means war.
When he came in, it means war for the German people and the world.
But at any rate, the Brazilian deal didn't materialize fast enough, and Mr.
Sachs didn't feel any great-- Martin Sachs.
S-A-C-H-S.
God rest his soul.
But anyway, Martin Sachs didn't work fast enough and didn't feel too hurried.
He led a somewhat isolated life.
And it was very pathetic, personally, at the table, when he would say, Fraulein-- I don't remember her name-- Fraulein Miller spoke to me today on the street.
And that was a former close employee of his, I don't know, secretary or something.
And she had had the courage to speak to him on the street.
And that was really worth telling to Anna-- Fraulein Annie, the governess, and his daughter, and me, and usually a guest of the family, someone there.
And it was very pathetic, you know, that meant so much to him.
And Fraulein Annie told me, the governess, I have a real problem.
You know, he loves Annamarie.
That was the apple of his eye, his Annamarie, his little 14-year-old daughter.
She doesn't like to have him take a walk with her.
She doesn't want to be seen.
He was a noted man in the town, the richest man.
Biggest taxpayer in Herschberg, he told me.
And lied-- probably that was his misfortune, you know.
But he didn't realize that.
He thought that was a contribution.
He had done his best for the politicians.
[CHUCKLES] But at any rate, this was, for Fraulein Annie, very difficult because little Annamarie, she had to tell her, now, you must go with your father.
Every day, the family-- at least Fraulein Annie and little Ana Marie, and if I were-- while I was there, I went with them-- went to the mother's grave and said a prayer.
The mother's grave was in a beautiful grave there called the Cavalierberg.
Probably still there, the Cavalierberg.
But without Herr Sachs, because of course he didn't get buried.
But at any rate, Herr Sachs.
I left.
I left because the school now had many, many students.
And they said, come back, or you'll lose their job.
It was a threat.
They would have taken me back anyway.
But I quickly went back to my weekly pay.
Herr Sachs had not had the courage to give me any money.
They gave me nice presents, and I was taken care of there, and I was learning German.
It was worth my while since I-- the school was in such a dark situation.
But I now had the chance to go back to school.
Come right back, or we can't keep your job for you.
So I went right back.
And they went to Berlin with me and bought me a pretty coat.
That was all they could do because his bank was so carefully watched.
And a Jewish man, which they regarded him, was not allowed to give money to anyone.
You know, it was his money, but at any rate.
Anyhow, that's how it was.
They went together with me to Berlin, partway to Hamburg, on my way back to school.
And we had a very nice time there.
And they brought me this nice coat, the three of them.
Fraulein Annie and little Annamarie and Herr Sachs.
He had business.
He always had business in Berlin with the consulate.
And you know, the people of Berlin didn't escape very well.
Hitler killed 35,000 of the people.
Dr.
Robert Kempner has written an interesting chapter about the Jews of Berlin.
And the reason I feel-- and also this was bad for Herr Sachs-- they felt quite secure.
There were many of them together.
They still could go out and get a cup of coffee in these great big restaurants.
Berlin is a particularly, what should I say, convivial, friendly city.
People are always going out for something in big groups.
And they felt secure, you know?
And the Berliners were very nice.
You know?
Later they proved quite courageous, some of the Berliners, you know, as much as they dared.
And they didn't feel fear-- enough fear.
There were too many of them together.
Who would dream that Hitler had such projections, whether he already knew the details himself of how he was going to do this.
But who would dream it's possible to-- to kill 35,000 people?
You don't do that.
We hadn't heard of a war.
What's the technique of that, you know?
Whether he had his technicians working on it or worked on it himself never occurred to anybody.
He hates us, but we're not in any real danger.
So they didn't move fast enough, many of them.
And Herr Sachs was among them.
And so they said goodbye to me.
I went to Hamburg and went back to my teaching job.
And they told me, you lost weight, that's fine.
Because I'd been-- done a lot of walking down there, where I had no duties with Herr Sachs and lost quite a lot of weight.
My colleagues told me, you've lost a lot of weight.
You look fine, you know, from my-- my treatment I had in a vacation down there with-- Because none of them learned English, I'm sorry to say.
I helped the little girl with her Latin now and then, but that didn't interest them too much.
And they might go to Brazil anyway.
Although he asked Annamarie, would you mind the snakes in Brazil?
They discussed whether that would be good.
But at any rate, she thought Brazil might be nice, even if there were snakes.
You know?
And they were planning.
But about three weeks before-- it was about August 15, 1939.
You know, the war began September 1.
Herr Sachs came through.
He was on his way to pick up Annamarie.
She was up at a coastline, a beach area up north of Hamburg, and he was going to pick her up, and he wanted to see me.
And he wanted my expert opinion.
I was 23 and probably politically unbelievably stupid.
You know?
And he said, do you think there will be war?
And I said-- this is on my conscience, of course, for all my life.
Stupidity it was.
It was no evil intention.
I thought, this poor man.
Why should he go out of Germany.
That Hitler, they'll get rid of him before too long.
You know?
And here he gives everything up to Hitler and goes off.
You know.
I thought--
And I didn't believe a war was coming.
That's how badly informed you can be two weeks before.
But many people were like that.
Many Germans.
Many people just didn't think.
Hitler's doing everything fine.
He threatens the people, but he's not going to get us into war.
You know, they thought Hitler's too smart for that.
Because everyone had the view stupidity to start a war, you know.
But Hitler was a very limited man in his knowledge of geography.
I think-- some of my students too, they had no training in geography.
If I'd say, well, New York is as far away from Hamburg as San Francisco is far away from New York as New York is from Hamburg.
They didn't believe that.
They didn't think I'd learned-- learned it right in school, you know.
For them, America was a funny little country, over there.
My students, you know.
And for Adolf, apparently, also.
But I told Mr.
Sachs as we walked around the Alster.
He was still trying to lose weight, and I didn't mind getting some exercise.
It was a Sunday afternoon, and he was going up to pick little Annamarie up and bring her back home, and the-- and the governess.
And he took-- we took this walk, and he said, well, he said, I-- I worry if I think there might be a war.
And I had listened with him on January 30 to Hitler's speech a few months-- six, seven, eight months before, when Hitler said, but if there is war, all the Jews of Europe will be wiped out.
And that he didn't like the sound of, that was very clear.
And he was beginning to believe these things.
There was enough suffering.
He suffered enough in Herschberg with the people afraid to say hello to him on the street and his little daughter cringing if he wanted her to go for a walk with him, you know.
And he said, I thought there was a war coming.
And I said, oh, I really think that's just bluff.
I said, he couldn't want a war.
So that was what I told him.
And he thought, how could he think it, that I would know anything about these things, you know what I mean?
No political science.
No-- you know, no knowledge of anything except my little college courses and my teaching English and so forth.
You know?
And he went away and said he would keep in touch, and I should call him up.
If I had time, I could come down again, and they'd like some more lessons.
But not urgent in any way.
And he went away, and I didn't see him again.
And-- but suddenly, the consulate called me up, the American consulate in Hamburg, and said, you get out of Germany.
He said, we can't be responsible.
This is about a week before the war.
Get out of Germany right away.
I said, but I have no money.
I didn't want to burden my poor American family back here.
They didn't have much.
And I was on this career binge, you know, if you're getting into a background for a teaching job.
And I didn't want to have them sending me money, and from where?
And you know, I didn't want to burden them any further.
And I could take 10 marks out of Germany.
What can you do with that?
And he said, well, by yourself.
I said, I don't think there will be a war.
Well, he said, I think there will be.
But he said, you get a round trip ticket from Hamburg to Amsterdam and return.
I had a little money from teaching and so forth, but it was German money.
And he said, then if it blows over, as you hope, come back the next week or come back in a few days.
It was at a critical point.
Hitler was cursing the British, and the British were-- it was a very tense moment, the last 10 days before the war.
You know?
About 10 days before the war started, I sat in a garden cafe in Hamburg with a couple of colleagues.
And the extra, there were always extras.
Extra, extra, and we snapped the extra up.
And it said, 'Poland threatened--' in German of course-- 'Poland threatens the destruction of Germany.' And here's this big headline.
You know?
A two-page special out, you know.
And of course, everybody knew that Poland couldn't destroy Germany, you know, at least we knew that much.
And Poland [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Poland threatens destruction of Germany.
And that was kind of a warning.
We learned later in the Nuremberg documentation, which I never expected to be into, that Hitler had [INAUDIBLE] what do we say?
He had organized that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] threat, allegedly, of the Poles.

And some German intelligence man had-- dirty tricks man-- had gone over and blown up a-- a Polish truck inside Poland, near the border, showing that Poland was against Germany, you see, and was about to go to war with Germany, little Poland.
But at any rate, I did what the consulate told me.
And my friend-- I left everything.
I took just a little bit of a suitcase.
I didn't have much else.
But I thought, I'll come right back.
I have no money.
And so I telegraphed my parents that I was going to Poland, would they please send me some money to American Express Company in Amsterdam.
And they did.
But I had no money when I left Germany.
And I'll tell you something that may shock you, but it's really funny from my point of view, but it may not be funny to anyone else after all we've gone through.
We had a terrible train ride.
It was the last train.
The reason I got on the train that Saturday afternoon, evening, was because the radio said, Holland will close the border at midnight tonight from people coming out of Germany.
And I thought, well, it's time for me to get over there then.
And [INAUDIBLE] helped me.
I packed a few little things together.
And I left everything else; my little room and all my little bit of possessions which I had there.
And [INAUDIBLE] came to the train with me.
And she said, I have a feeling we won't see each other for a long time.
And she waved goodbye to me at the railway station.
And I thought, oh, I'm coming back next week, I hope.
But anyway, I went to Amsterdam.
But as I crossed the border, I wonder if I should tell you what happened.
It was really, to me, funny, and I survived it.
But it's not very-- maybe many people may pick it up in a very-- after all we've been through, in a way, that it really wasn't-- sincerely, it really wasn't.
We had a hell of a ride on the train out from them.
Stopping.
And it was full of people who had come probably, I don't know where, from Czechoslovakia, whose little possessions seemed to be-- I don't know who they were.
There was only standing room on this train.
We stood so tight in that train, you couldn't fall over in any direction.
You were exhausted when you got to the Dutch border.
And we came across the Dutch border and stopped at this little town inside for the customs and the Dutch people to look us over.
And I got out, and I said, oh, here come the customs and the Dutch police.
And I said, oh, heil Hitler.
I had never said heil Hitler before in Germany.
We didn't have to, we foreigners.
Our Herr Director said, now you don't have to say heil Hitler, but behave in a seemly way.
Don't-- don't make yourself obnoxious.
Don't-- we didn't want his business spoiled, his school business.
But I was so happy to be in a free country again, that I just-- more or less, we were all exhausted, you know.
But the Dutch police, they didn't take that.
I thought that's just humor, you know.
But they didn't take it in any funny way at all.
They didn't like that at all.
You know?
And it was really just spontaneous to hell with Hitler, you know, heil Hitler.
I was just dippy.
You know?
And they thought, well, are you a Nazi?
I said oh no, I'm an American.
But it didn't help much.
I had kind of blackened myself in their eyes.
And they said, do you have any money?
And I said, well, I have just my 10 marks, but my family in America is going to send me, I'm pretty sure, money at the American Express in Amsterdam.
Now they said, you go back.
You go back to Germany.
And I thought, you go back to Germany?
That ride behind me and back to Germany.
And I said, but really, that was just my idea of a joke.
It's a bad joke, I admit, but-- [LAUGHS] And they made me go back into Germany.
Maybe because I had no money, but I think they took a kind of dark view of me.
What kind of a politician was I?
Well anyhow, just stupid all the way through.
But back I went about, oh, two kilometers inside the border.
They said, there's a hotel over there.
And when you get word that the money is in Amsterdam, then we will let you through.
Well, the war was oncoming.
Even I could see it.
You could see enormous fires inside the border of Holland, two-- two kilometers away.
And the Dutch, I believe, had burned all their enormous oil supply, so the Germans wouldn't get them, you know?
Along the border, there were these huge oil refinery or supplies of oil inside Holland, and apparently didn't want the Germans to get them.
And there were enormous, beautiful fires against the sky.
All night long, you see from this little, simple hotel, wooden hotel porch, you could see the fires burning in-- in-- not in Amsterdam, in Holland.
And the next day, there was a couple other people there that also wanted to get into Holland.
They said, we'd better go.
They'll let us in now.
So we went across the border again, and we were taken off the train.
We saw the Dutch training.
They were in training then.
And they-- they looked so sloppy compared to these German martinets that had been marching around the streets all these years.
And they have a football under there.
It was a little free time for them.
They were kind of sloppy, a little bit overweight, not trained at all.
They were marching around the streets.
All the Dutch had been mobilized.
And they have always a football under their arm, and they planned to have a good time, you know?
They didn't take the war seriously either, I guess.
But at any rate, very kind.
The Dutch seemed to be very kind, religious people.
Took us under their wing.
And I said, but I'm not.
I was so honest in my stupidity.
I said, but I'm not a Jewish refugee.
That's all right, dear, you come with us too.
And so we all went and were treated so kindly because we-- mobilization.
We had been pushed off the train inside Holland because the Army had the train.
The Dutch army was mobilizing.
So they took us into a hotel in this little town.
And this religious group-- I don't know what sector they belonged, what sect they belonged to-- but they were so kind.
And they took us hungry, tired, bedraggled, frightened people who had been pulled off our railway train, into the hotel.
Gave us a nice, clean bed.
And the next day, they gave us a breakfast I will never forget.
I don't know.
There must have been 15 different kinds of bread on it and spreads and cuts of meat and unbelievable the way they treated us.
And I felt bad, because I thought, I haven't been persecuted.
I may be a fool, but I didn't-- my own fault, my-- my suffering, you know?
And they just treated us so kindly.
And then they let us take our tickets in a couple days and get onto the train again.
And my ticket was for Amsterdam.
And when I got there, I-- oh, they didn't want any pay, the religious people that were-- we take care-- it was some church group-- we take care of the hotel, don't worry about that.
They fed us royally and allowed us then to go back on the rest of our trip to Amsterdam.
Get off there, and go our different ways.
And I got my little bit of money from my parents.
And then the problem was war was declared about the next day.
I think it was-- I think it was the 1st of September, war-- Hitler declared war.
And a couple days later, England declared war.
And we went down to the Amsterdam bulletin board, always, to see what the latest headlines were.
So much Dutch we could also command, you know?
Then the problem was to get out of Europe.
How could you get out of Europe?
Everybody wanted to get out of Europe.
Everybody who belonged in the Western hemisphere who had a chance to go for some other reason, you know.
Where do you get a ship?
There were no airplanes in those days.
So I finally had the good fortune to get a third class ticket on the Statendam, a Dutch ship, for the end of October.
This was the beginning of September.
And by then I wanted to get home.
It was-- we knew it was war, and a job is not a job if there's a war there.
To Inge I said-- when we parted at the train, I said, if there is a war, I don't want to be bombed by American bombers.
You know, and she said she often thought of that.
She was in Hamburg during the war.
She stayed with her fiance.
And she often thought of that when the bombers were up there.
And remember what I had said, I don't want to get bombed by American bombers.
[LAUGHS]
So I gradually got home on the last day of the World's Fair in October 30.
My father and my brothers picked me up and back to a normal world, and.
But the people I left behind, I left in a very tragic situation.
Before we go any further, I'd like to ask you, what did you know about the concentration camps while you were still in Germany?
How much did you actually know?
Kristallnacht.
Kristallnacht.
No.
No.
Hitler didn't spread that around too much.
And even the people involved were perhaps not too proud of it, you know what I mean?
But it was knowable, excepting that I had practically no German, and we also had practically no time to read the newspapers or anything.
You know what I mean?
The-- the students, primarily Jewish people that made this such a successful school with their desire to learn English, and other languages too, French or-- or Spanish, for Latin America.
They didn't say much about it.
Hm?
Oh, you wouldn't like what he told me.
Are we on duty here?
I don't know.
He said, the Jews didn't behave so well, he said.
You know.
[CHUCKLES] He, of course, was a professional non-Jew, the poor man.

I forgot to tell you that not only did he regard himself as not a Jew, the poor, unfortunate man, but he also was very puzzled that his church, so-called church, the Lutheran church, when he went to the different helpful committees in Berlin, would have nothing to do with him.
And so he was.
He couldn't go to the Jewish.
There were a little bit of Jewish organized help for one another.
You know?
He couldn't go to them, really, for help, and he couldn't go to anyone.
You know?
He was a man-- and he was such a proud, loyal German.
He was so loyal to Germany.
And he couldn't go to the Germans.
He really was in a terrible position, the poor man.
You know?
When you came back to the States, what did you tell your family and friends about conditions in Germany?
Ooh, everything I knew, [CHUCKLES] of course.
I even gave little lectures around the area.
But when I went to Berlin, coming back to Hamburg from Herr Sachs, they right away wanted to know everything.
I learned more from them, the American consular people, the embassy in Berlin.
Because I thought, maybe I can get a job at the embassy.
That would be nice.
So I went and talked to them and told them of my experiences.
And they wanted to know everything that I had seen and heard.

I could tell them that Herr Sachs was a man in a very difficult position, and that he was trying to get out, but that that would mean great sacrifice of everything he had worked for all his life, to leave most of it behind.
And that he had been in the concentration camp and had told that there were great ordeals there.

I guess they would not let-- at that stage, in that concentration camp action in Kristallnacht-- they would not let the people out until they signed a kind of promise that they would get out of Germany as soon as they could.
At that moment, Hitler wanted them out of Germany.
Later, he didn't want them out of Germany, and he prevented them from leaving Germany.
He wanted them obliterated, and he thought they were bad propaganda for him when they came out to France, to America, you know.
But at the consulate, they were deeply interested, and our ambassador had already gone home in protest of this Kristallnacht.
And they said, oh, we could hire you right away if you could read German script.
But of course, among other things I couldn't do, I couldn't read this German writing.
We had so many letters from people in German script.
But unfortunately I had to say, I can read Latin writing, but, you know, but I can't read German script.
So I went back to Hamburg and taught, and then came the war.
No, I don't.
But I do know, very sadly, he was a mountaineer.
I mean, people love to walk in the mountains.
And Herschberg is right near the wonderful Riesengebirge, a wonderful set of mountains.
Wonderful.
And he took us all, the family, he took us up on a mountain trip one time.
And it was partly to reconnoiter, because he was a very good mountaineer.
And he gave me some of his shoes.
You have to have shoes.
And I-- my feet were swollen as big as a bushel, you know, from-- from climbing up in these heavy shoes.
I didn't have proper shoes at this time.
And we climbed and climbed.
I think, I don't know, a day and a half, till we got to something called the schneekoppe.
It's a peak in the Riesengebirge that looks down in the moonlight, when the moon is shining, to Czechoslovakia.
At that time, a free country.
And he wanted to reconnoiter exactly how he would be able, if any danger came, to go up.
He had already been in the concentration camp.
To go up.

And he kept a little suitcase packed at one door in his house, so that if they [INAUDIBLE] again on the door, on the front door, he told Fraulein-- Fraulein Annie had her instructions, and he would disappear out the back door with his packed little suitcase and would go into the schneekoppe and bounce down.
Very athletic.
I mean, a trained-- mountain trainer-- trained man in the mountains, go down into Czechoslovakia and be free.
In the meantime, Hitler had taken Czechoslovakia, I believe, just about the-- shortly after we did our reconnoiter job.
You know?
So it wouldn't have done the poor man any good.
And of course, he realized that, to get up there and go down into Hitler's Czechoslovakia, you know?
Do you know what ultimately happened to Mr.
Sachs?
Yes.
He died in Auschwitz.
I learned that-- I knew, of course, it must be true.
But I learned that because he was so prominent, he was a target man, among other things, for them.
He was a-- you see, Hitler, I'm sure, had this marvelous dream for himself.
These rich Jews, I'll take all their ownings and their property, and I can run a wonderful war.
You know?
And they didn't have.
There weren't that many Jews, and they didn't have that much money, of course.
But this was certainly one that they thought they could get a lot out of.
And they took a lot out of them all with a big fine that Hitler leveraged right after this Kristallnacht.
So they took a great deal.
But they were after him.
And I knew that he must have died.
But his little daughter survived.
She was also in a concentration camp as a so-called mischling, mixed Jewish-German background, antecedents.
And she too was in a concentration camp.
And she escaped-- survived, shall we say-- and came, and you've read about these treks they made.
